text: Here we present a video of robotic ampulectomy for a peri-ampullary neoplasm.

Our patient is a fifty nine year old female who was evaluated for upper abdominal pain of four months duration which was associated with loss of appetite and non quantified weight loss. Ultrasound of abdomen showed a dilated CVD with soft tissue mass in the distal one-third. The blood investigations were normal.

MRCP showed a peri-emplary neoplasm with dilatation of both the bile and the pancreatic ducts. PET-CT scan showed a hypermetabolic soft tissue density in the peri-emplary region with ductal dilatation and no FDG-avid lymph nodes. While the above investigations were pointing towards malignant etiology, interestingly, biopsy showed tumor with low to high-grade dysplasia.

So after counseling, the patient was planned for robotic amplectomy with frozen section biopsy and V-pulse procedure if indicated. The surgery was performed in DaVinci X robo. Ports were placed as shown in the picture. The patient was placed in reverse Tenderlenburg position.

The surgeon begins the procedure by dissecting the gastrocolic ligament to mobilize the transverse colon inferiorly. The patient is positioned in reverse Trendelenburg to facilitate exposure. The omental adhesions to the gallbladder and liver are freed, continuing the dissection laterally until the hepatic flexure is fully mobilized.

The surgeon uses prograsp forceps to retract the duodenum upwards, freeing its lateral attachments and carefully dissecting it away from the inferior vena cava. The robotic system facilitates precise dissection in the confined space.

The surgeon localizes the ampulla, identifying the large growth through tactile feedback. A duodenotomy is performed on the lateral wall using an ultrasonic energy device, then extended longitudinally with hook electrocautery, ensuring the incision is made at the ampulla level to avoid excessive length.

The surgeon pulls the ampulla through the duodenotomy, placing stay sutures to keep the duodenal wall open for retraction. Ampullary transduction begins in the inframedial aspect using an ultrasonic energy device, deepening the dissection. Pancreatic juice flows from a dilated pancreatic duct, and a stitch is placed between the pancreatic duct and duodenal mucosa to prevent retraction and aid traction. The dissection continues superiorly, with visible bile flow from the bile duct.

The surgeon observes a dilated bile duct and pancreatic duct. The specimen is placed in an endo bag and removed for a frozen section biopsy. Following a negative biopsy result for high-grade dysplasia or invasive malignancy, the procedure continues with the ampulectomy.

The surgeon begins the reconstruction of the common bile duct (CBD) by suturing the superior aspect of the CBD to the duodenal mucosa using PDS-C-C sutures. A bridging anastomosis is performed between the CBD and the pancreatic duct.

They can be treated with endoscopic papillotomy, transduodenal ampullectomy, or pancreatic duodenectomy. However, compared to endoscopic papillotomy, transduodenal ampullectomy offers higher chance of complete resection. The ductal reconstruction is completed by taking interrupted sutures all around.

The surgeon uses a robotic approach for transduodenal ampullectomy, placing stay sutures over the duodenum to assist in closing the duodenotomy. A blue EndoGIS stapler is used for closure, with the duodenotomy being closed transversely to prevent stricture formation.
A surgical drain is placed in the right subhepatic space, and the omentum is positioned over the duodenotomy site.

Patient was started on oral chips on post-op day two. The drain was removed on day five and patient was discharged on day seven. The final histopathology report was intra-ampullary papillary tubular neoplasm with low-grade dysplasia with negative margins. These are rare neoplasms of the ampulla of Werther. They are biologically indolent but can follow adenoma carcinoma sequence. Even though invasive lesions follow malignant course, they have better prognosis than typical ampullary carcinoma.
Thank you for watching the video.
